perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees to notify the Discloser in writing of any misuse or misappropriation of such Confidential Information which may come to its attention.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient is not required to notify the Discloser in writing of any misuse or misappropriation of such Confidential Information which may come to its attention.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires the recipient to notify the discloser of any misuse of confidential information. The modified text removes that obligation, potentially conflicting with duties of good faith or reasonable care depending on the jurisdiction and circumstances.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Implied Duty of Good Faith
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the requirement to notify the Discloser of misuse or misappropriation, the Recipient is potentially acting in bad faith and hindering the Discloser's ability to protect its confidential information. This contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing present in contracts, which requires parties to act honestly and reasonably in performing their contractual obligations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the implied duty of good faith. The original text required the recipient to notify the discloser of misuse of confidential information, which aligns with acting honestly and reasonably. The changed text removes this requirement. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines good faith as encompassing 'honest dealing' and 'faithful performance of duties'. Removing the notification requirement directly undermines the discloser's ability to protect confidential information and implies a lack of honest dealing, satisfying the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: Any materials or documents of which have been furnished by the Discloser to the Recipient will be promptly returned, accompanied by copies of such doc umentation, after the evaluation set forth in Section 1 of this Agreement has been concluded.
perturbation.changed_text: Any materials or documents of which have been furnished by the Discloser to the Recipient will be returned within 180 days, without any copy of such documentation, after the evaluation set forth in Section 1 of this Agreement has been concluded.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires 'prompt' return of materials. Changing this to 'within 180 days' may be too long and conflict with reasonable timeframes depending on the context and potentially violate trade secret laws if the delay allows for reverse engineering or unauthorized use. Also removing the requirement for copies to be provided increases the amount of time before the company can use their own documents.
perturbation.law_explanation: Extending the return period to 180 days without copies gives the recipient more time to potentially misappropriate trade secrets or confidential information, contradicting the intended protection provided by trade secret laws.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions U.S. Code Notes prev | next As used in this chapter— (1) the term “ foreign instrumentality ” means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term “ foreign agent ” means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of a foreign government; (3) the term “ trade secret ” means all forms and types of financial, business, scientific, technical, economic, or engineering information, including patterns, plans, compilations, program devices, formulas, designs, prototypes, methods, techniques, processes, procedures, programs, or codes, whether tangible or intangible, and whether or how stored, compiled, or memorialized physically, electronically, graphically, photographically, or in writing if— (A) the owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret; and (B) the information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another person who can obtain economic value from the disclosure or use of the information; (4) the term “ owner ”, with respect to a trade secret , means the person or entity in whom or in which rightful legal or equitable title to, or license in, the trade secret is reposed; (5) the term “ misappropriation ” means— (A) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means ; or (B) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who— (i) used improper means to a

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a potential contradiction related to trade secret law. The original text mandates a 'prompt' return, while the changed text specifies 'within 180 days.' The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest that this extended timeframe, coupled with the removal of the requirement to provide copies, could allow the recipient to misappropriate trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' as the 'acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means' and defines 'trade secret'. By extending the return period without copies, the changed text increases the risk of such misappropriation, which would undermine the protections intended by trade secret laws, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The foregoing commitments shall survive any termination of discussions between the parties, and shall continue for a period of three (3) years following the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The foregoing commitments shall survive any termination of discussions between the parties, and shall continue for a period of ten (10) years following the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text sets a 3-year confidentiality period. Extending this to 10 years may be deemed unreasonable in some jurisdictions, especially if the information's competitive value diminishes significantly over time. This could be seen as an unenforceable restraint of trade.
perturbation.law_explanation: Extending the confidentiality period to 10 years could be seen as an unreasonable restraint of trade if it prevents the Recipient from using general knowledge or skills acquired during the evaluation period, especially if the confidential information becomes outdated or publicly available.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: restraint of trade | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you restraint of trade A restraint of trade is any activity that tends to limit a party's ability to enter into transactions. The term is most commonly used in the context of government antitrust regulation. For example, federally, 15 U.S.C. § 1 prohibits “[e]very contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations.” For example, in American Needle, Inc. v. Nat’l Football League , the U.S. Supreme Court held that licensing activities for individual teams’ intellectual property conducted through a corporation separate from the teams with its own management [i.e. the NFL] fell within a possible restraint of trade covered under 15 U.S.C. § 1. Many states also have their own local restraints on anti-competitive business activity. For example, Massachusetts General Law Ch. 93 § 4 states “[e]very contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce in the commonwealth shall be unlawful.” [Last reviewed in April of 2025 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities unfair competition wex definitions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends a confidentiality period from 3 years to 10 years. The Perturbation Explanation suggests that this extended period may be an unreasonable restraint of trade. The Law Explanation states that such an extension could be an unreasonable restraint if it prevents the recipient from using general knowledge or skills acquired during the evaluation period. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'restraint of trade' as any activity that tends to limit a party's ability to enter into transactions. Extending the confidentiality period significantly could be seen as limiting the recipient's ability to use information or skills, thus constituting a restraint of trade under the definition provided.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the internal laws of the United Kingdom, and shall be binding upon the parties hereto in United Kingdom and worldwide.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the internal laws of North Korea, and shall be binding upon the parties hereto in United Kingdom and worldwide.
perturbation.explanation: Specifying North Korean law as the governing law may be unenforceable or against public policy, especially if it conflicts with fundamental legal principles or human rights standards in the UK or other relevant jurisdictions. UK courts may refuse to enforce provisions that violate their public policy.
perturbation.law_citation: Dicey, Morris & Collins, The Conflict of Laws (15th ed. 2012)
perturbation.law_explanation: Choosing North Korean law as the governing law for a contract between UK-based entities raises significant public policy concerns. UK courts are unlikely to enforce a contract governed by a legal system that is fundamentally incompatible with its own legal and ethical standards. The UK has established laws relating to sanctions and the use of DPRK law will violate UK jurisdiction.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The change from United Kingdom to North Korea introduces a significant legal contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight that choosing North Korean law raises public policy concerns and potential conflicts with UK legal and ethical standards, potentially violating UK jurisdiction. The original text ensures the contract is governed by a legal system compatible with the UK. The changed text replaces it with a system likely to be incompatible and unenforceable in the UK, creating a direct conflict with the original intent and potentially UK law. Snippet 2 mentions that conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute. The change here introduces a jurisdiction whose laws are likely to be in conflict, creating a legally problematic contract.
